author: @pauljs75
text: The earthquake thing Tesla fiddled with took a good bit of mass to do its thing (the moving part wasn't exactly lightweight), and it also has to hit the resonant sympathetic frequency of the structure it was attached to. Conceptually it's simple enough though, even if it being something "portable" takes breaking down the weights making up the mass being used. I think most people covering the story got the frequency part right, but they overlooked what it took in terms of impulse to do its thing.

author: @ch5139
text: To discover that even a slowing pendulum has the exact same period as when it started must have been a lightbulb moment to whoever first noted it, it still seems counterintuitive. I know someone who has a passing interest in old clocks and even they didn’t know that about pendulums, not surprising though as a clock pendulum gets a ‘kick’ to keep the same stroke.

author: @Biggles732
text: There was a time when we used to speak of 17 jewell watch or 21 jewell watch or whatever number. The more jewells was associated with better quality. I believe that the bearings were the supposed little jewells and shaped to hold the tiny spindles. The early electronic watches were called quartz watches , and it confused people who couldn't see how tiny bearings could be fabricated from basically grains of sand . I should finally check what particular mineral those little jewell bearings was .

replies.author: @Natethesandman1
replies.text: Corundum: synthetic sapphire or ruby. See the wiki entry for Jewel bearing

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ruby was sometimes used as well

author: @jamesross1003
text: Another one for the curio shelf.  Itching to play with the mechanism myself.  Robert, you need to give us a tour of the designs and builds you have done over the years, especially those didn't make the cut for a video.  Thanks so much Robert!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering No doubt about that.  It's why I would love to see it if you should still have them.  A "Here is my journey" would be a great video idea.  I am more interested in the thought progression process of making a device than I am the actual end product.  I guess the various points of progress would be a better way of putting it.  It might make for an easy video planning idea for you.  I also loved the museum videos that you did. The dark oxygen discovery would maybe be a good video.  Thanks for the response!

author: @ΜικεΡ
text: Not only do pendulums not really work for wrist watches, but imagine you're on a ship rolling from side to side. The center on the escapement mechanism rotates relative to gravity, ultimately throwing off the 'speed' of the period from the clock's perspective. (Like the clock can't tell it's listing side to side, it can only tell when the pendulum passes through it's centerline)

replies.author: @Biggles732

replies.author: @bearnaff9387
replies.text: Like an escapement, it allows you to meter out rotational energy stored into a spring into discrete "beats".  It's just a tool for doing a job.  If the escapement is a hammer, this is a mallet.
